text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
STAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with newly identified right renal mass on outside imaging. Presenting complaint of right flank pain and weight loss. Evaluation for extent of disease prior to multidisciplinary oncology consultation.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed utilizing a three-phase protocol (corticomedullary, nephrographic, and excretory phases) following administration of 100 mL of intravenous iohexol contrast. Reconstructions in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes were performed at 2.5 mm slice thickness. A low-dose CT of the chest was performed without contrast prior to the abdominopelvic acquisition. Scout images obtained. No significant motion artifact. Image quality: diagnostic throughout.

COMPARISON: Outside ultrasound report referenced; no prior cross-sectional imaging available for comparison.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are hyperinflated with mildly increased AP diameter, consistent with mild chronic obstructive changes in the appropriate clinical setting of remote tobacco use. No lobar consolidation. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.

Pulmonary nodules: Multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules are identified, the morphology and distribution of which are highly concerning for hematogenous metastatic disease in the setting of a known renal primary. These include:
  - Right lower lobe: a well-circumscribed, smoothly marginated soft tissue nodule measuring 1.9 cm in its greatest axial dimension (series 3, image 214). The nodule has a homogeneous appearance with no internal calcification or cavitation.
  - Right upper lobe: a smaller subpleural nodule measuring 1.1 cm, right posterolateral upper lobe (series 3, image 112). Also smooth and non-calcified.
  - Left lower lobe: a 0.8 cm nodule in the posterior basal segment, similar morphology (series 3, image 198).
  - Left upper lobe: a 0.6 cm nodule in the left upper lobe, anterolateral (series 3, image 88).
  No additional pulmonary nodules are identified above the 6 mm threshold. No satellite lesions. No endobronchial lesions.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is midline. The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy identified above the 1.0 cm short-axis threshold. The main pulmonary artery is not enlarged. No filling defect within the main pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism on this study (though the examination was not specifically protocoled for pulmonary embolism).

Chest wall and soft tissues: No osseous destructive lesion involving the thoracic spine, ribs, sternum, or clavicles. Mild thoracic spondylosis at multiple levels, age-appropriate. No axillary lymphadenopathy.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth hepatic contour. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously with no discrete focal lesion identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. No perihepatic fluid. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder and biliary tree: Gallbladder is present and unremarkable without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or cholelithiasis. Common bile duct within normal limits at 4 mm.

Spleen: Normal in size, approximately 10.5 cm in craniocaudal dimension. Homogeneous splenic parenchyma without focal lesion.

Pancreas: The pancreas is visualized through its entirety, including the uncinate process. Pancreatic parenchyma is unremarkable with no ductal dilatation, no pancreatic mass, and no peripancreatic stranding.

Adrenal glands: Left adrenal gland: morphologically normal, no nodularity. Right adrenal gland: appears within normal limits on this examination; no discrete nodule identified.

Kidneys:
  Left kidney: Normal in size, shape, and position. Homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi. No mass identified.
  Right kidney: There is a large, predominantly exophytic, heterogeneous mass arising from the mid-to-lower pole of the right kidney, measuring approximately 11.4 cm in greatest axial dimension (11.4 x 9.8 x 10.2 cm on coronal reformats). The mass demonstrates avid arterial phase enhancement with washout on delayed imaging, a pattern highly characteristic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma. Extensive central geographic hypodensity is consistent with large areas of tumor necrosis. The peripheral viable tumor demonstrates a characteristic, heterogeneous enhancement pattern with prominent internal vascularity. The tumor extends beyond the renal capsule with perinephric fat stranding and soft tissue thickening, indicating T3 involvement. There is no discrete contiguous involvement of the ipsilateral adrenal gland, though the right adrenal is displaced superiorly.

Renal vein and inferior vena cava: Critical finding: the right renal vein is expanded and filled with hypoattenuating tumor thrombus extending from the renal sinus into the infrarenal inferior vena cava. The IVC thrombus measures approximately 3 cm in craniocaudal extent within the IVC lumen, beginning just below the renal vein ostium. The thrombus is non-occlusive; contrast opacification is seen surrounding the filling defect within the IVC. The thrombus does not extend to the level of the hepatic veins. The left renal vein is patent. No supra-hepatic or intrahepatic IVC extension.

Retroperitoneal lymph nodes: Multiple enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes are identified, highly suspicious for nodal metastatic disease:
  - Right para-aortic/right lateral aortic chain: the dominant node measures 2.8 cm in short axis (series 5, image 178), with heterogeneous internal enhancement and irregular margins. Several adjacent smaller right para-aortic nodes measure 1.0-1.5 cm in short axis.
  - Aortocaval region: an aortocaval nodal mass measuring 2.2 cm in short axis (series 5, image 165), with similar morphology.
  - Right common iliac chain: a 1.5 cm right common iliac lymph node (series 5, image 245).
  - No significant left para-aortic, left iliac, or pelvic lymphadenopathy identified.
  - The overall nodal burden is most prominent at the right para-aortic and aortocaval stations, anatomically consistent with right-sided renal primary drainage.

Other abdominal structures: No free intraperitoneal fluid. No peritoneal implants identified. Bowel loops are unremarkable without evidence of obstruction or wall thickening. No mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Aorta is of normal caliber throughout.

PELVIS:

Urinary bladder: The bladder is partially distended and unremarkable in appearance. No intraluminal lesion, wall thickening, or perilesional inflammatory change. No filling defect.

Uterus and ovaries: The uterus is appropriately atrophic in appearance for the patient's age. No uterine or adnexal mass identified. No significant pelvic free fluid.

Bony pelvis: No lytic or blastic osseous lesion within the sacrum, ischii, iliac wings, acetabula, or proximal femora. Moderate degenerative changes at the right greater trochanteric region.

Pelvic lymph nodes: No pathologically enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. LARGE RIGHT RENAL MASS: A heterogeneous, avidly enhancing 11.4 cm right renal mass with extensive internal necrosis is identified. The imaging features are highly characteristic of clear cell renal cell carcinoma. The tumor extends beyond the renal capsule into the perinephric fat (T3 or greater staging).

2. RENAL VEIN AND INFRARENAL IVC TUMOR THROMBUS: Tumor extends into the right renal vein and into the infrarenal inferior vena cava over approximately 3 cm. The thrombus is non-occlusive. Extension does not reach the level of the hepatic veins. These findings are consistent with pT3b disease.

3. RETROPERITONEAL LYMPHADENOPATHY: Multiple enlarged right-sided retroperitoneal lymph nodes including the dominant right para-aortic node (2.8 cm), aortocaval node (2.2 cm), and right common iliac node (1.5 cm), morphologically consistent with metastatic nodal involvement.

4. BILATERAL PULMONARY NODULES: Four bilateral pulmonary nodules ranging from 0.6 cm to 1.9 cm in greatest dimension are identified. In the clinical context of a large vascular renal primary, these are consistent with pulmonary metastatic disease. The largest lesion is located in the right lower lobe (1.9 cm).

5. NO HEPATIC, OSSEOUS, OR ADRENAL METASTATIC DISEASE identified on this examination.

6. The overall imaging findings are compatible with Stage IV (M1) renal cell carcinoma with locoregional nodal involvement, IVC tumor thrombus, and bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease. Clinical correlation and multidisciplinary oncologic evaluation are appropriate.
